title: Each thought, a leaf on a river
subtitle: A visualisation for releasing thoughts at bedtime
excerpt: A ten-minute bedtime visualisation for the busy mind that won't let sleep arrive. Each thought that surfaces is placed on a leaf and sent down a river. Not suppressed. Released.
sourceNotes: The leaves-on-a-river metaphor appears in Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) and mindfulness traditions. Framing and script original to homemade.education.

practice.whenToUse: In bed when thoughts are arriving faster than sleep and the mind needs a gentle release mechanism rather than suppression

content.text: A ten-minute bedtime visualisation for the mind that won't quiet down. Trying to stop thoughts doesn't work. This technique changes your relationship to them: each thought that surfaces gets acknowledged and released, rather than grabbed and extended.
Lie down. Close your eyes. Take three slow breaths, longer on the exhale. Let the body be heavy. You are not going to force sleep. You are creating the conditions.
In your mind, find a river, slow-moving and a little dappled. The banks are quiet. There are fallen leaves drifting past. You are sitting on the bank, watching.

As thoughts arrive, and they will, don't push them away. Instead, take each one and place it on a leaf. Name it briefly: "tomorrow's meeting," "the thing I forgot," "whether I said the right thing." Place it on the leaf. Watch the leaf carry it downstream. Then return your eyes to the water and wait for the next one.
When you notice you have been pulled into a thought, following it rather than releasing it, notice that, name it once ("thinking"), and return to the bank. This is not failure. It is the practice.
The leaves on a river image is used in ACT and mindfulness practice. This script is original to homemade.education.
